coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of pH-responsive fluorescent carbon dots using waste leather scrap for anti-counterfeiting
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Wenbo
Li, Wen
Wang, Qi
Zhang, Junfeng
Li, Li
Zhou, Zhijun
Lyu, Bin
Ma, Jianzhong
coredata.dc:description: The fabrication of carbon dots (CDs) with biomass materials has become one of the research hot spots. Waste leather scrap (WLS) has been proven to be practicable for the preparation of CDs with simple fluorescence properties for anti-counterfeiting because of their cheap and environmentally responsible characteristics. For a more complex and harder imitation of anti-counterfeiting, stimuli-responsive CDs with WLS as raw material are urgently needed. Herein, acid-responsive fluorescent carbon dots (L-CDs) and alkaline-responsive fluorescent carbon dots (H-CDs) were prepared from WLS via a simple one-step solvothermal/hydrothermal method with emission wavelengths of 457 nm and 549 nm, respectively. When decreasing or increasing the pH value, their emission wavelengths change to 633 nm and 424 nm, respectively. The CDs are highly fluorescence reversible (20 times) in a broad pH region. The detailed results indicated that CDs have the structure of graphite carbon and possess wealthy chemical groups, like CO, –OH, C–N, etc. These are the main reasons for the change of emission wavelength. Due to the stable pH-responsiveness, the CDs were employed as fingerprint, ink, and figure code in anti-counterfeiting. The pH-responsive fluorescent CDs using WLS for anti-counterfeiting enrich the process of solid waste with a sustainable, cost-effective method for practical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Waste leather scrap
Carbon dots
pH-responsive
Anti-counterfeiting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of acid responsive fluorescent-carbon dots (L-CDs)
Preparation of alkaline responsive fluorescent-carbon dots (H-CDs)
Morphology and structure characterizations of CDs
Optical responsive mechanisms to pH and their application

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of L-CDs (a) and H-CDs (b); HRTEM images of L-CDs (c) and H-CDs (d); XRD patterns (e) and Raman spectra (f) of CDs (the insets are the particle size distribution diagrams).
XPS spectra of L-CDs (a) and H-CDs (d); High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s for L-CDs (b) and H-CDs (e); high-resolution XPS spectra of N1s for L-CDs (c) and H-CDs (f).
Optical images of CDs under daylight (a) and 365 nm UV light illumination (b).
PL spectra of L-CDs (a) and LL-CDs (d); the CIE color coordinates of the L-CDs (b) and LL-CDs (e), emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths of L-CDs (c) and LL-CDs (f).
PL spectra of H-CDs (a) and HH-CDs (d); the CIE color coordinates of the H-CDs (b) and HH-CDs (e), emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths of H-CDs (c) and HH-CDs (f).

Trend plots of fluorescence intensity of L-CDs, LL-CDs (a), H-CDs and HH-CDs (c) at different pH values; the reversible fluorescence response to pH of L-CDs (b) and H-CDs (d) varied iteratively between different pH values.
Fluorescence quantum yields of L-CDs (a), LL-CDs (b), H-CDs (c), and HH-CDs (d).

Fluorescence intensities of L-CD (a), LL-CD (b), H-CD (d) and HH-CD (e) at different concentrations of NaCl (the inset is an optical image of CD); and comparison of fluorescence intensities of L-CD (c) and H-CD (f) after addition of different interfering ions.
UV–vis spectra of L-CDs and LL-CDs (a), H-CDs, and H-CDs (b), Zeta potentials of L-CDs and LL-CDs (c), H-CDs, and H-CDs (d), FT-IR spectra of L-CDs and LL-CDs (e), H-CDs, and H-CDs CDs (f).
Photographs of fluorescent anti-counterfeiting fingerprint (a), ink (b), and figure code (c) based on CDs.
Schematic diagram of the L-CDs and H-CDs synthesized from WLS.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of pH-responsive fluorescent carbon dots using waste leather scrap for anti-counterfeiting

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shaanxi University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The fabrication of carbon dots (CDs) with biomass materials has become one of the research hot spots. Waste leather scrap (WLS) has been proven to be practicable for the preparation of CDs with simple fluorescence properties for anti-counterfeiting because of their cheap and environmentally responsible characteristics. For a more complex and harder imitation of anti-counterfeiting, stimuli-responsive CDs with WLS as raw material are urgently needed. Herein, acid-responsive fluorescent carbon dots (L-CDs) and alkaline-responsive fluorescent carbon dots (H-CDs) were prepared from WLS via a simple one-step solvothermal/hydrothermal method with emission wavelengths of 457 nm and 549 nm, respectively. When decreasing or increasing the pH value, their emission wavelengths change to 633 nm and 424 nm, respectively. The CDs are highly fluorescence reversible (20 times) in a broad pH region. The detailed results indicated that CDs have the structure of graphite carbon and possess wealthy chemical groups, like CO, –OH, C–N, etc. These are the main reasons for the change of emission wavelength. Due to the stable pH-responsiveness, the CDs were employed as fingerprint, ink, and figure code in anti-counterfeiting. The pH-responsive fluorescent CDs using WLS for anti-counterfeiting enrich the process of solid waste with a sustainable, cost-effective method for practical applications.

The pH-responsive fluorescent CDs were prepared by a solvothermal/hydrothermal method using waste leather scraps as raw material, which could be applied for anti-counterfeiting with their good fluorescence property.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Counterfeit behavior is an increasingly severe global problem, causing a serious threat to enterprises, governments, and consumers. Countries and governments spend a lot of money on counterfeiting to protect products and consumers. Developing new technologies for anti-counterfeiting to make authenticity easier to identify and more difficult to copy is very important for protecting the legal rights of consumers. Therefore, up till now, anti-counterfeiting technology has gradually developed, such as laser anti-counterfeiting [1], ink anti-counterfeiting [2], magnetic security anti-counterfeiting [3], etc. In particular, carbon dots (CDs) are widely used in anti-counterfeiting research and application owing to their many advantages, such as easier modification [4–6], luminescent performance [7–9], good biocompatibility [10–12], high fluorescent stability [13], and easy synthesis process [14].

Most studies have realized anti-counterfeiting by changing the pH value to control the fluorescent intensity or fluorescent color of CDs. For fluorescent intensity, some studies have used non-biomass materials to prepare CDs. Chen et al. [15] synthesized CDs from p-phenylenediamine and the result shows that fluorescent intensity decreased sharply at pH 1.0–3.0. Moreover, fluorescent intensity can be recovered successfully when the pH value is adjusted to 7.0. Guo et al. [16] developed d-histidine and glucose derived CDs nanozymes with superoxide dismutase-like activity and hydroxyl radical scavenging activity in novel smoke purifier for free radical scavenging and pungent odor masking. Biomass materials were good choices for the preparation of pH-responsive fluorescent CDs, such as bombyx mori silk [17], grape seed extract [18], sugarcane extract [19], sodium alginate [20], lignin [21], and folic acid [22]. Zhao et al. [23] utilized kelp for the preparation of CDs via microwave irradiation and found that when the pH of the solution increase from 3.0 to 8.0, the fluorescence intensity decreased from 650 to 500 gradually. CDs were synthesized from black fungus by Qin et al. [24], while the emission intensity is the strongest when the pH value is 4, and the fluorescence intensity is reduced when the pH value is changed. In addition, these researches, which use aspartic [25], l-tryptophan [26], herbal medicine [27], chitosan [28], wine lees [29], and glucose [30] as precursors of CDs, have demonstrated that proteins or amino acids contained biomass materials are highly suitable for the production of CDs. Our previous work also demonstrated that CDs can be prepared with waste leather scrap (WLS) [31]. However, it is not obvious and not aesthetical enough to achieve anti-counterfeiting changes in the fluorescence intensity.

A small number of studies have realized anti-counterfeiting by changing the pH value to regulate the fluorescent emission wavelength of CDs [32]. For non-biomass materials, for instance, CDs have been produced using o-phenylenediamine and polyethylene glycol as raw materials by Xia et al. [33]. The phenomenon is that the fluorescent color changes from white to yellow as the pH value increases. Fan et al. [34] processed graphite rods in the sodium toluenesulfonic acid acetonitrile solution by electrolysis to get CDs. The prepared CDs emit green fluorescence in an aqueous solution with a pH below 6.8. However, the emitted fluorescence switched to blue when pH increased to above 6.8. For biomass materials, Zhang et al. [35] synthesized CDs with the assistance of a microwave from aspartic acid and the result shows that the CDs display an obvious color change from blue to green form pH 2 to 12. Song et al. [36] used l-cysteine as a carbon precursor to prepare CDs, which varied color from blue at 2.01 to green when the pH was 10.83. Most importantly, the CDs showed good reversibility eight times after acid-base cycle treatment. The change of fluorescent color of these CDs is easy to observe and enhances the anti-counterfeiting effect. Unfortunately, the emission wavelengths of CDs in most studies are short, and the fluorescent colors are blue and green. Few studies have used biomass materials to prepare pH-responsive fluorescent CDs for excellent reversibility of a wide range of colors, thus limiting the anti-counterfeiting application.

Leather-based industries play an irreplaceable role in economic development, bringing significant economic benefits for the national economy [37]. However, it is inevitable that a lot of WLS are produced in the production process. The common treatments of WLS are stacking and incineration, which is a fearful waste of resources and pollution of the environment. Therefore, it is worth considering the appropriate approach to WLS. WLS contains a considerable amount of protein, primarily composed of elements of carbon, hydrogenium, oxygen, and nitrogen. This composition makes WLS a suitable carbon source for the preparation of pH-responsive fluorescent CDs. Not only does it bring excellent economic benefits for the leather industry but it also is the key to the leather industry to dealing with the problem of the environment. As previously mentioned, using WLS to prepare pH-responsive fluorescent CDs can simultaneously realize waste utilization and improve the anti-counterfeiting effect.

This study focuses on the preparation of pH-responsive fluorescent CDs by a solvothermal/hydrothermal method with the use of WLS as an environmentally responsible carbon source and the modification of the CDs by doping nitrogen and phosphorus sources to acid-responsive fluorescent carbon dots (L-CDs) and base-responsive fluorescent carbon dots (H-CDs) as shown in Scheme 1 . The fluorescence properties of CDs were used to accurately analyse its structure and pH response mechanism. The CDs can be used to fingerprint, ink and number codes in anti-counterfeiting applications.

The hydrochloric acid solution (HCl (36 %)) was obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Sodium chloride (NaCl), NaOH solution (1 M), o-phenylenediamine (OPD), m-phenylenediamine (MPD), 2-amino terephthalic acid, ethanol, phosphoric acid, copper chloride, ferrous chloride, calcium carbonate, sodium nitrite, dipotassium hydrogen phosphate, manganese chloride tetrahydrate, zinc nitrate hexahydrate, magnesium sulfate, barium chloride and microporous membrane were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd. Poly (vinyl alcohol) (PVA; average molecular weight: 500 g/mol) was purchased from Shanghai Tixiai Co. Ltd. Analytical grade is used for all chemicals. It can be used directly without further processing for experiments. WLS was donated by Zibo Dahuanjiu Polygrace Tannery Co. Ltd. Ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.1 MΩ cm was used for all experiments.

As a first step, the WLS was washed with 150 mL of NaCl solution (8 % by weight) after being weighed at 10.0 g. The mixture was stirred magnetically (300 rpm) at 30 °C for 10 h. In the next place, the WLS was dried and ground to a powder with a mesh size of 80 using a high pass vibratory ball mill. Finally, 5 g of powders were placed in 50 mL of HCl (4 mol/L) for 5 h, and the temperature was kept at 90 °C. A tan solution of 0.24 g/mL can be obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of acid responsive fluorescent-carbon dots (L-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: L-CDs were prepared by solvothermal method. In the first place, 0.69 g of OPD, 30 mL of ethanol, 0.0525 g of 2-amino terephthalic acid, and 6.9 mL of WLS solution were added into a beaker. The mixture was stirred until a uniform suspension was formed. Secondly, the uniform suspension was transferred to a PTFE tank and sealed with a stainless steel reactor. Then the reactor was moved to an oven of at 180 °C for 6 h. After cooling, the solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for approximately 15 min. Finally, L-CDs dispersions were obtained by vacuum filtration using a filter membrane. The yield of L-CDs was 99 % with the method of measuring the weight of the resulting precipitate and filtration slag. The LL-CDs were obtained by using an HCl solution (36 %) to make sure the pH of the solution was kept at 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of alkaline responsive fluorescent-carbon dots (H-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: H-CDs produced by a hydrothermal method. First, a homogeneous suspension of 0.41 g of MPD, 15 mL of phosphoric acid, 4.07 mL of WLS solution and 2 mL of water was prepared by stirring. Second, the suspension was moved to an oven with a stainless steel reactor of a PTFE tank at 200 °C for 24 h. The purification step was identical to that employed for L-CDs in order to acquire H-CDs. The yield of (H-CDs) was calculated to be 99 % by weight of the resulting precipitate and filter residue. The HH-CDs were obtained by using NaOH solution (1 M) to adjust the pH of the solution to 13.

The crystalline structure of CDs was tested by an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, D/max2200PC, Japan) at a scan rate of 0.02° 2θ s−1 in the range of 10–50°. Chemical groups of CDs were measured with a Fourier-transform infrared instrument (FT-IR, Vertex70, German), and the spectral range was 4000-500 cm−1. Measuring the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, AXIS Supra, England) with an Al Kα source. All the samples were placed on a Si plate for the XPS test. The morphology, structure, and size of CDs were detected by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN, USA) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). Optical performance was received by UV–vis spectrum (UV–Vis, Cary 5000, USA). Photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL, Edinburgh FS5, England) spectra and quantum yield (QY) were measured with an excitation wavelength of 250–800 nm. Specific test methods are described in the Supplementary Information. Measuring the graphitization degree of CDs by Raman scattering spectra (THEM DXRxi, USA), and the scattering range was 1000–1800 cm−1. The size and Zeta potential of CDs were obtained from dynamic laser scattering (DLS, Malvern Zetasize, England). Elemeraor organic element analyzer (VarioELcube, Levitra, Germany) was used to obtain the elements of CDs. A high frequency vibrating ball mill (AM100S, China) was used to produce the WLS powder.

Firstly, 2 g of PVA and 30 mL of water were added to a beaker and stirred at 85 °C until the PVA colloidal solution was clear. When the solution is cooled to room temperature, 10 mL of diluted CDs dispersion at a concentration of 0.01 g/mL is added to 5 mL of PVA solution, and then warmed to 25 °C and stirred for 30 min. The product was used as a fingerprint, ink and digital code in anti-counterfeiting.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure characterizations of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology, structure and functional groups of CDs play an irreplaceable role in changing the emission wavelength. The size, morphology, and structure of CDs were measured by DLS, TEM, HRTEM, XRD, and Raman. Fig. 1 a and b are the TEM images of L-CDs and H-CDs which showed a uniformly distribution with the diameters of 4.0 nm and 8.0 nm, respectively. The results are also consistent with DLS. HRTEM images in Fig. 1c and d shows a clear crystal structure of CDs and a lattice spacing of 0.230 nm corresponding to the graphitic carbon (110) plane [38]. Fig. 1e shows the XRD patterns of the synthesized CDs, which can be seen to have a broad peak at 26° associated with the graphite structure [39]. The Raman characterization of CDs is shown in Fig. 1f. Band D at 1380 cm−1 corresponds to the disordered carbon of CDs and the band G at 1570 cm−1 corresponds to graphitic carbon [40]. The relative I D/I G intensities of the L-CD and H-CD were computed to be 1.46 and 1.02, respectively. Based on the preceding discussion, it can be inferred that WLS holds a significant position due to its elevated carbon content of 42.29 % in the production of CDs.

The composition and bonding configuration of CDs were measured by XPS. As shown in Fig. 2 a–d, the full XPS spectra presents four predominant peaks at 284.6 eV, 399.6 eV, 531.6 eV, and 98.6 eV, which are ascribed to carbon (C), nitrogen (N), oxygen (O), and silicon (Si), respectively [41–43]. L-CDs and H-CDs were placed on a silicon wafer for testing, so trace amounts of silicon were detected, which does not affect the results of the analysis. In addition, obvious peaks at 133.6 eV can be observed for H-CDs, which correspond to phosphoric acid because of raw material. At the same time, XPS spectra of C1s and N1s were deconvoluted for the interfacial bonding investigation of CDs. In Fig. 2b–c, the XPS spectra of C1s and N1s of L-CD were split into three peaks at 284.57 eV corresponding to sp2 C (C–C/CC), 285.66 eV corresponding to sp3 C (C–O/C–N), and 288.62 eV corresponding to CO bonding, which proves the presence of a distinct graphitic structure and abundant chemical groups on the surface [44]. The N1s spectrum is convolved into three distinct peaks corresponding to pyrrolidine N at about 399.13 eV, graphite N at 400.09 eV, and amino N at 400.9 eV [45]. For H-CDs, the C1s band is decomposed into three peaks corresponding to sp2 C (C–C/CC) at about 284.62 eV, sp3 C (C–O/C–N) at 286.07 eV, and CO bonds at 288.74 eV. The peaks of graphite N at 398.93 eV and amino N at 400.87 eV confirm that the N element is perfectly bound to CDs, as shown in Fig. 2e–f.

Fig. 3 presented that, in daylight, the H-CDs solution is nearly transparent, the L-CDs and HH-CDs solutions are yellowish-brown, and the LL-CDs solution is pink. Nevertheless, under 365 nm, L-CDs, LL-CDs, H-CDs, and HH-CDs are blue, red, kelly, and bluish violet, respectively. To confirm the basic properties and optical response to pH of the as-synthesized CDs, fluorescence spectroscopy was measured. Fluorescence emission and excitation spectra of the L-CDs and LL-CDs are illustrated in Fig. 4 a–d. As the solution changes from neutral to acidic condition, the emission wavelength increases from 457 nm to 633 nm. At the same time, the CIE coordinates of L-CDs and LL-CDs are (0.1559, 0.1575) and (0.706, 0.2939), respectively, as shown in Fig. 4b–e. As shown in Fig. 5 a–d, with the increasing pH value, it is seen that the emission wavelengths were 549 nm and 424 nm for H-CDs and HH-CDs, respectively. The CIE coordinates are (0.4067, 0.528) and (0.1696, 0.0909), respectively, in Fig. 5b–e. The results reveal that CDs possess significant pH-dependent performance. The CDs show wide colors at different pH, which could give an obvious color signal to evaluate the pH. Besides, the emission spectra under different wavelengths of excitation were measured (shown in Fig. 4c, f and 5c, 5f) and indicated excitation wavelength independence.

As shown in Fig. S1, when the pH value decreased from 7.0 to 2.0, the emission peak at 457 nm gradually decreased, while a new emission peak was formed at 633 nm, and the fluorescence color of L-CDs changed from blue to red. The emission wavelength of H-CDs varied between 549 nm and 424 nm (kelly to blue-violet) as the pH increased from 7 to 13. Further as shown in Fig. 6 a–c, the fluorescence intensity trends of L-CDs and treated LL-CDs and H-CDs and treated HH-CDs, respectively. What's more, the fluorescence intensity decreased slightly even after 20 times of acid-base cycling treatment with NaOH and HCl solutions (Fig. 6d–d). The above phenomena indicate that these processes are highly reversible over a wide range of low and high pH values. Therefore, it is a promising anticounterfeit fluorescent material.

As exhibited in Fig. 7 a–d the fluorescence quantum yields of L-CDs, LL-CDs, H-CDs, and HH-CDs dispersion are calculated to be 11.00 %, 0.58 %, 9.12 %, and 16.10 %, respectively. Fluorescent stability is a vital element for estimating the capability of fluorescent CDs in a variety of environments. Thus, the fluorescence stability of CDs in a NaCl solution has been investigated. Even at concentrations as great as 1 mol/L of NaCl, the stability remains intact, and the PL intensity is still nearly unaltered, as shown in Fig. 8 a, b, 8d and 8e. Optical images of CDs showed no significant decrease in fluorescence intensity, suggesting that CDs can accept high ionic intensities. The phenomenon will not affect the application of anti-counterfeiting. In order to investigate the anti-interference ability of L-CDs and H-CDs to common ions in the environment, more common cations and anions at 0.2 M were added to L-CDs and H-CDs, respectively, as shown in Fig. 8c–f. The interfering effect of ions was very small in comparison with that of blank samples, indicating that they have good anti-interference ability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical responsive mechanisms to pH and their application
ce:section.ce:para: Investigation of the mechanism of pH-responsive fluorescence of CDs, UV–vis, Zeta potential, and FT-IR were further applied to estimate CDs. Fig. 9 a–b shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of the as-synthesized CDs. All CDs have a distinct absorption peak at around 200 nm, which matches to the π-π* electronic transition of the CC bond of the sp2 carbon clusters. What deserves attention is the altered position of the peaks of the CDs. Compared with L-CDs, LL-CDs show a prominent new peak at 509 nm, which may be due to the n-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 structure containing CN/CO bonds or Mie scattering from the CDs, which represents the typical absorption of an aromatic system and indicates extended conjugation in the CD structure. Compared with H-CDs, the 427 nm peak at HH-CDs disappeared revealing a change in the structure [46–49].

Fig. 9c shows the Zeta potential of L-CDs and LL-CDs, which are −5.8 mV and 4.8 mV, respectively. Under acidic conditions, the amine and carboxylic groups are mostly protonation [50] The amino group in the protonated state is more active than the unprotonated amino group in neutral or alkaline conditions and thus may lead to high polymerization of carbon dots. The conjugated structure of the sp2 domain and the increased graphitic nitrogen content favour the red shift of the emitting wavelength so that the emission wavelength red-shift of LL-CDs to 633 nm can be attributed to the resulting new surface states and extended conjugate structure. However, these conjugated structures are prone to inducing the aggregation of carbon dots through the π-π* stacking effect, which results in reduced fluorescence intensity. Meanwhile, it is known that protonation of aromatic carboxylic acids also leads to fluorescence red-shift and decreased fluorescence intensity, causing changes in fluorescent properties. H-CDs and HH-CDs exhibit negative potentials of −6.8 mV and −7.5 mV. As pH increases, amine and carboxylic groups are deprotonated, and part of the carboxyl functional group is converted to -COONa, reducing the number of –COOH at the periphery of the sp2 conjugate domain. The emission wavelength transform 549 nm–424 nm, and the other part is deprotonation to -COO-, forming a negatively charged “protective shell” around the carbon core to enhance the fluorescence intensity [51].

Fig. 9e–f exhibit the FT-IR spectra of CDs. The stretching vibration peak at 3448 cm−1 is observable, indicative of several hydrophilic groups with –NH2 or –OH in L-CDs [52]. The C–N bonds were formed at 1407 cm−1, and the peak value is 1641 cm−1, which is attributed to CC [53,54]. The assimilation peaks at 2990 cm−1 are attributed to the vibrations of C–H. By contrast, the bands at 1050 cm−1 and 1720 cm−1 correspond to the vibrations of C–O–C and CO appeared for LL-CDs, and the vibration intensity of C–N at 1407 cm−1 is decreased distinctly, which may be the reason for long emission wavelength and low fluorescent intensity. For H-CDs, the peaks of the stretching vibration at 3491 cm−1 are identified, indicating a variety of hydrophilic groups with –NH2 or –OH in L-CDs. The C–N bonds were formed at 1410 cm−1, and the peak value is 1658 cm−1, which is due to CC. The absorption peaks at 2918 cm−1 are due to C–H vibrations. By contrast, the bands at 1410 cm−1 was distinctly increased for HH-CDs, the vibrations of C–O at 1014 cm−1 are disappeared, which may be the reason for high fluorescent intensity and short emission wavelength. In summary, protonation, deprotonation and changes of surface functional groups are the primary reasons of different emission wavelength were confirmed by UV–vis spectra, Zeta potential, and FT-IR.

On the basis of the above analysis, the synthesis of CDs makes them a promising tool for anti-counterfeiting measures thanks to their favorable optical properties. They can be utilized in fingerprinting, ink, and figure code applications. As depicted in Fig. 10 , in daylight, all images are nearly invisible. CDs can be used for the detection of fingerprints, as shown in Fig. 10a. Simultaneously, CDs can be used to detect inking, under 365 nm irradiation, images exhibit the characteristics of “SUST” with blue and kelly colors. After the treatment of HCl and NaOH solutions, the photographs become red and bluish-violet colors. In Fig. 10c, five-pointed stars and creative materials are also changed from blue and kelly colors to red and bluish-violet colors. Meanwhile, a digit pattern was painted by marking the black parts. At 365 nm, on the one hand, the photograph displayed a blue digit “11720548”, after decreasing the pH value by HCl solution, the actual numbers of the red digit “11110”. On the other hand, the kelly digit “10168752” transformed to the bluish violet digit “0017”, which is the actual information. These phenomena indicate that CDs can be used as a promising anti-counterfeiting material.

In summary, acid responsive fluorescent-carbon dots and alkaline fluorescent responsive-carbon dots have been successfully produced from waste leather by a simple synthetic route using waste leather scrap. The structure of CDs was characterized in detail. It is found that the change in functional group is the primary cause of the change in emission wavelength. Notably, the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased slightly after 20 acid-base cycles. Finally, illustrating the mechanism of CDs for different emission wavelength and applying to fingerprint, ink, and figure code for anti-counterfeiting. The results show that the pH-responsive performance of the CDs better improves the anti-counterfeiting effect, making authenticity easier to identify and harder to duplicate. This study outlines a method for employing WLS in the production of CDs, facilitating the anti-counterfeiting of CDs and developing a solid foundation for the anti-counterfeiting.
The datasets used and/or analyzed during the current study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

We thank the Natural Science Basic Research Program of Shaanxi (Program No. 2024JC-YBMS-122); National Natural Science Foundation of China (21908141, 52073164) and the Basic Industrial Science and Technology Projects of Wenzhou (G20190008).

Wenbo Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Wen Li: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Qi Wang: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Junfeng Zhang: Writing – review & editing. Li Li: Writing – original draft, Conceptualization. Zhijun Zhou: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Bin Lyu: Writing – review & editing. Jianzhong Ma: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2024.112431.
